narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 97% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. This high risk score flagged your profile as one where the school felt you might struggle to succeed long-term. Looking at your background, you ranked in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack standing—while your undergraduate GPA of 2.60 and LSAT score of 24 also fell well below the averages of 3.21 and 36.70 seen in admitted students. As a full-time Asian female student from a middle-income family, your demographics aligned somewhat with the majority, but they didn't offset the academic concerns. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your low Year 1 grade decile, which had the single biggest influence in pushing the prediction toward bar failure, followed closely by your similarly low Year 3 decile. Your LSAT score came next in importance, further tipping the scales against you, with family income level and racial background playing smaller but still negative roles, then your undergraduate GPA, full-time status, and gender having the least sway. In essence, these academic underperformers stood out as the top-ranked factors compared to peers who typically pass the bar, where stronger early grades and test scores are common predictors of success. While your middle-income background is right around average and didn't help much, it wasn't a major drag either. To improve your odds for future applications or bar success, focus on realistic steps like boosting your study habits to climb higher in grade deciles next time, retaking the LSAT to aim closer to the 37-point average, and building a stronger academic record overall through targeted prep or additional coursework. Many students in similar spots have turned things around by seeking tutoring or bar-specific practice early. You're not defined by this one prediction—plenty of paths lead to passing the bar with persistence. Keep pushing, and consider reapplying after showing these improvements.
